ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To Virgilius, Bishop.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Gregory to Virgilius, Bishop of Arelate (Arles).

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: O how good is charity, which through an image in the mind exhibits what is absent as present to ourselves, through love unites what is divided, settles what is confused, associates things that are unequal, completes things that are imperfect!

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Rightly does the excellent preacher call it the bond of perfectness; since, though the other virtues indeed produce perfectness, yet still charity binds them together so that they can no longer be loosened from the heart of one who loves.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Of this virtue, then, most dear brother, I find thee to be full, as both those who came from the Gallican parts and the words also of thy letter addressed to me testify to me of thee.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now as to thy having asked therein, according to ancient custom, for the use of the pallium and the vicariate of the Apostolic See, far be it from me to suspect that thou hast sought eminence of transitory power, or the adornment of external worship, in our vicariate and in the pallium.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, since it is well known to all whence the holy faith proceeded in the regions of Gaul, when your Fraternity asks for a repetition of the old custom of the Apostolic See, what is it but that a good offspring reverts to the bosom of its mother?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: With willing mind therefore we grant what has been asked for, lest we should seem either to withdraw from you anything of the honour due to you, or to have despised the petition of our most excellent son king Childebert.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But the present state of things requires the greater earnestness, that with increase of dignity solicitude also may advance, and watchfulness in the custody of others may grow, and the merits of your life may serve as an example to your subjects, and that your Fraternity may never seek your own through the dignity accorded you, but the gains of the heavenly country.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For you know what the blessed apostle says, groaning, For all seek their own, not the things which are Jesus Christ’s (Philip. ii.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For I have learnt from information given me by certain persons that in the parts of Gaul and Germany no one attains to holy orders except for a consideration given.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: If this is so, I say it with tears, I declare it with groans, that, when the priestly order has fallen inwardly, neither will it be able to stand outwardly for long.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For we know from the Gospel what our Redeemer in person did; how He went into the temple, and overthrew the seats of them that sold doves (Matth. xxi.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For to sell doves is to receive a temporal consideration for the Holy Spirit, whom, being consubstantial with Himself, God Almighty gives to men through the imposition of hands.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: From which evil what follows is already intimated.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For of those who presumed to sell doves in the temple of God the seats fell by God’s judgment.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And in truth this transgression is propagated with increase among subordinates.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For he who is promoted to any sacred order for a price, being already corrupted in the very root of his advancement, is the more ready to sell to others what he has bought.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And where is that which is written, Freely ye have received, freely give (Matth. x.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, seeing that the simoniacal heresy was the first to arise against the holy Church, why is it not considered, why is it not seen, that whoso ordains any one for money, causes him in advancing him, to become a heretic?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Another very detestable thing has also been reported to us; that some persons, being laymen, through desire of temporal glory, are tonsured on the death of bishops, and all at once are made priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: In such cases it is already known what manner of man he is who attains to priesthood, passing suddenly from a lay estate to sacred leadership.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And one who has never served as a soldier fears not to become a leader of the religious.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: How is that man to preach who has perhaps never heard any one else preach?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Or how shall he correct the ills of others who has never yet bewailed his own?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, where Paul the apostle prohibits a neophyte from coming to sacred orders, we are to understand that, as one was then called a neophyte who had been newly planted in the faith, so we now reckon among neophytes one who is still new in holy conversation.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Moreover, we know that walls after being built, are not made to carry a weight of timber till they are dried of the moisture of their newness, lest, if a weight be put on them before they are settled, it bear down the whole fabric together to the ground.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, when we cut trees for a building, we wait for the moisture of their greenness to be first dried out, lest, if the weight of the fabric is imposed on them while still fresh, they be bent from their very newness, and be the sooner broken and fall down from having been elevated prematurely.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Why, then, is not this scrupulously seen to among men, which is so carefully considered even in the case of timber and stones?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: On this account your Fraternity must needs take care to admonish our most excellent son king Childebert that he remove entirely the stain of this sin from his kingdom, to the end that Almighty God may give him the greater recompense with Himself as He sees him both love what He loves and shun what He hates.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And so we commit to your Fraternity, according to ancient custom, under God, our vicariate in the Churches which are under the dominion of our most excellent son Childebert, with the understanding that their proper dignity, according to primitive usage, be preserved to the several metropolitans.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: We have also sent a pallium for thy Fraternity to use within the Church for the solemnization of mass only.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Further, if any one of the bishops should by any chance wish to travel to any considerable distance, let it not be lawful for him to remove to other places without the authority of thy Holiness.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: If any question of faith, or it may be relating to other matters, should have arisen among the bishops, which cannot easily be settled, let it be ventilated and decided in an assembly of twelve bishops.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, if it cannot be decided after the truth has been investigated, let it be referred to our judgment.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now may Almighty God keep you under His protection, and grant unto you to preserve by your behaviour the dignity that you have received.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Given the 12th day of August, Indiction 13.
